meta.name: Jacob Schaal
meta.bio:
I research how artificial intelligence transforms labour markets and what policy responses can protect workers — particularly those at the start of their careers. My work on “seniority-biased technological change” examines why AI disproportionately automates entry-level positions, threatening traditional career progression pathways.

I serve as AI Governance Research Manager at Cambridge ERA, Research Assistant at King’s College London under Dr Bouke Klein Teeselink, and Contributing Editor of the AI Economics Brief at Windfall Trust. I hold an MSc in Economics from the London School of Economics.

policy.coAuthors: D. Muller, L. Leung, M. Chen
policy.description: Policy report presented at AISI and Department for Business and Trade. Strong accept at AIES 2025. Also published as 'Enabling AI Safety Information Sharing' at EuRIPS 2025.

academic.title: Enabling AI Safety Information Sharing: UK Competition Law Block Exemptions and Institutional Design
academic.coAuthors: D. Muller, M. Chen, L. Leung
academic.description: Develops competition law block exemptions enabling frontier AI labs to share safety-critical information without antitrust violations. Proposes AISI as neutral clearinghouse.

academic.title: AI Automation Exposure Index Based on Moravec’s Paradox
academic.description: Theory-based index classifying 19,000+ O*NET tasks across four automation dimensions.

academic.title: The Costs and Benefits of Advanced AI: Evidence From the Labour Market
academic.description: BSc thesis supervised by Prof. Ines Helm (Chair of AI in Economics). Grade: 1.0 (highest distinction).

academic.title: AI’s Labour Market Effects in the UK
academic.description: Empirical research with Dr Bouke Klein Teeselink using Revelio Labs data, NLP task extraction, and triple-difference estimators.

researchInterests: My research centres on the economics of AI, particularly how automation affects labour markets across experience levels. Core themes include: seniority-biased technological change, AI automation exposure measurement, the future of vocational training, and AI governance and regulation (EU AI Act, UK AI Safety).
